problem: A can complete a piece of work in 10 days and B can complete the same work in 5 days. In how many days can they complete the work together?

conversation.speaker: student
conversation.text: I think this is about adding days? So 10 + 5 = 15 days?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Interesting start! Let me ask you—if B alone takes 5 days, does it make sense that together they would take 15 days?

conversation.speaker: student
conversation.text: Oh… no, that seems slower. They should be faster together.

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Exactly! So instead of adding days, we usually think in terms of work done per day. Can you tell me how much work A does in one day?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Think about it this way: if A finishes the whole work in 10 days, what fraction of the work does A complete in one day?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Yes! Great. Now, what about B? How much work does B do in one day?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Correct. Now if they work together, what should we do with these fractions—add or subtract?

conversation.speaker: student
conversation.text: Maybe subtract? Because days were reduced?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Think carefully—both are contributing work each day. Are we combining their efforts or canceling something out?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Good. Can you simplify that sum?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Nice work! So together they complete 3/10 of the work in one day. Now, how many days will it take to complete the whole work?

conversation.speaker: student
conversation.text: I think it's 10/3 days? That's like 3.33 days?

conversation.speaker: tutor
conversation.text: Exactly! Now, can you explain in your own words why we added the fractions and then took the reciprocal to find the total time?

conversation.speaker: student
conversation.text: Yeah… we first find how much work each person does in one day. Then since they both work together, we add their work per day. That gives total work done in one day. Then we flip it to find how many days are needed to finish the full work.
